The current parent (hill_autocatalysis, 0.9541) underperforms the best programs (0.9585-0.9589) which all use the surface-catalyzed secondary nucleation family with a time-gate. Since I'm asked to explore, but the best family is clearly the Knowles/Meisl mass-balance with maturation time-gate, let me think about what limits it.

The bottleneck across all top programs is shape_loss (~0.165) while NMSE is already ~0.002. The parent here has worse NMSE (0.0066) because it lacks explicit time-forcing — it's purely autonomous, so it can't move the inflection/crossing timing per dose. That's the key deficiency.

Let me check the average curve data to understand the actual dose-dependent shape behavior before committing.

Key observation: AUC decreases with dose (11.3 at cd=0 → 9.1 at cd=2e-6 for m0=3e-6 unseeded), and final_y is always 1.0 (plateau is dose-independent). So inhibitor delays/broadens but keeps the same endpoint. This confirms the top programs' structure: plateau inhibitor-independent, timing/width reshaped by dose.

The parent (hill_autocatalysis) is autonomous — no explicit time term. That's why it can't hit the delayed crossings and has higher NMSE. The best move is to give this diverse family an explicit maturation time-gate to move ignition timing while keeping its distinctive Hill-autocatalytic gate and additive source structure (which is structurally different from the top programs' multiplicative surface term). This preserves diversity while adopting the proven timing mechanism.

Let me quickly confirm the winning structural principle via a targeted lookup on secondary-nucleation inhibition.
